[Coordinates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [48°7′53″N 11°32′57″E / 48.13139°N 11.54917°E / 48.13139; 11.54917](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Oktoberfest&params=48_7_53_N_11_32_57_E_type:landmark)

[![Image 4: Listen to this article](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/20px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:En-Oktoberfest-article.ogg "Listen to this article")

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

World's largest folk festival

This article is about the original festival in Munich. For Oktoberfest celebrations around the world, see [Oktoberfest celebrations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_celebrations "Oktoberfest celebrations").

| Oktoberfest |
| --- |
| [![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/O%27zapft_is%21_M%C3%BCnchens_5_Jahreszeit_hat_begonnen_-_O%27zapft_is%21_Munich_5_season%2C_the_Oktoberfest_has_begun_%289855483374%29.jpg/250px-O%27zapft_is%21_M%C3%BCnchens_5_Jahreszeit_hat_begonnen_-_O%27zapft_is%21_Munich_5_season%2C_the_Oktoberfest_has_begun_%289855483374%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:O%27zapft_is!_M%C3%BCnchens_5_Jahreszeit_hat_begonnen_-_O%27zapft_is!_Munich_5_season,_the_Oktoberfest_has_begun_(9855483374).jpg) Theresienwiese during the 2013 festival |
| Observed by | [Munich](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Munich "Munich") |
| Type | Cultural |
| Celebrations | Parades, music, Bavarian food and beer |
| Date | September |
| 2026 date | 19 September - 4 October |
| Duration | 16–18 days |
| Frequency | Annual |
| Related to | [Oktoberfest celebrations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_celebrations "Oktoberfest celebrations") |
[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7e/Logo_Oktoberfest_Munich.svg/250px-Logo_Oktoberfest_Munich.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Logo_Oktoberfest_Munich.svg)

Oktoberfest logo

**Oktoberfest** (German pronunciation:[[ɔkˈtoːbɐˌfɛst]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")[](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/55/De-Oktoberfest.ogg/De-Oktoberfest.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:De-Oktoberfest.ogg "File:De-Oktoberfest.ogg"); [Bavarian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language"): _Oktobafest/d'Wiesn_) is the world's largest [Volksfest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volksfest "Volksfest"). It combines a beer festival with a fun fair and is held annually in [Munich](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Munich "Munich") on the [Theresienwiese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theresienwiese "Theresienwiese") from mid-September to the first Sunday in October.

The event draws around seven million visitors each year. In 2023, attendance reached a record 7.2 million.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-1) Visitors consumed approximately 7.4 million litres of beer.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-2) The festival features amusement rides, games, food stalls, and traditional Bavarian dishes.

The first Oktoberfest was held on 12 October 1810 to celebrate the wedding of Crown Prince [Ludwig](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria") and Princess [Therese of Saxony-Hildburghausen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Therese_of_Saxe-Hildburghausen "Therese of Saxe-Hildburghausen").[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-3) Since then, the festival has been cancelled on multiple occasions, most recently in 2020 and 2021 during the [COVID-19 pandemic in Germany](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Germany "COVID-19 pandemic in Germany").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins (1810–1811)

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins (1810–1811)")]

On 12 October 1810 Crown Prince Ludwig of Bavaria married Princess [Therese of Saxe-Hildburghausen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Therese_of_Saxe-Hildburghausen "Therese of Saxe-Hildburghausen"). Munich officials invited the public to celebrate on fields outside the city walls. The site was named [Theresienwiese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theresienwiese "Theresienwiese") (“Therese's Meadow”) the following year and is still called _Wiesn_.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-5)

The first festival featured a horse race modelled on the medieval _Scharlachrennen_ once run at the Karlstor. Major Andreas Michael Dall'Armi of the National Guard proposed the idea, although coachman Franz Baumgartner later claimed credit. The race was repeated in 1811, forming the core of the Oktoberfest tradition.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-6)

Sendlinger Hill—now _Theresienhöhe_—served as a natural grandstand for about 40,000 spectators. Sixteen pairs of children in regional costume opened the programme, thirty horses ran a 3,400 metres (11,200 ft) course, and a student choir closed the event. Baumgartner's horse won, and he received a gold medal from Minister of State [Maximilian von Montgelas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maximilian_von_Montgelas "Maximilian von Montgelas").

### 19th century

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![Image 7](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Adam_Pferderennen_Oktoberfest_1823.jpg/250px-Adam_Pferderennen_Oktoberfest_1823.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Adam_Pferderennen_Oktoberfest_1823.jpg)

Horse race at the Oktoberfest, 1823

*   **1811** – An agricultural show was added to promote Bavarian farming.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-7)
*   **1813** – The fair was cancelled during the [War of the Sixth Coalition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_of_the_Sixth_Coalition "War of the Sixth Coalition").
*   **1814** – Skittles, swings and climbing poles were introduced on its return.

Carnival booths appeared in 1818, offering prizes of silverware, porcelain and jewellery.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-8) Munich’s city council assumed control in 1819 and decreed that Oktoberfest be held annually.

A Greek delegation that visited in 1832 later cited the festival as a model for the [Zappas Olympics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zappas_Olympics "Zappas Olympics"), precursors of the modern Olympic Games.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-9)

During the century the opening was moved into late September to take advantage of warmer evenings; only the final days now fall in October.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-10)

#### Parades and monuments

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Parades and monuments")]

[![Image 8](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Carl_Kricheldorf_Portrait_eines_M%C3%A4dchens_in_Tracht.jpg/250px-Carl_Kricheldorf_Portrait_eines_M%C3%A4dchens_in_Tracht.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Carl_Kricheldorf_Portrait_eines_M%C3%A4dchens_in_Tracht.jpg)

Girl in a _Dirndl_

Since 1850 the annual [Trachten- und Schützenzug](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Trachten-_und_Sch%C3%BCtzenzug&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trachten- und Schützenzug (page does not exist)") (costume and marksmen parade) has marched from Maximilianstraße to the Theresienwiese, with about 8,000 participants led by the _[Münchner Kindl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M%C3%BCnchner\_Kindl "Münchner Kindl")_ mascot.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-11)

The bronze [Bavaria statue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bavaria_statue "Bavaria statue"), designed by [Leo von Klenze](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leo_von_Klenze "Leo von Klenze") and sculpted by [Ludwig Michael Schwanthaler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ludwig_Michael_Schwanthaler "Ludwig Michael Schwanthaler"), was erected in 1850 in front of the [_Ruhmeshalle_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruhmeshalle_(Munich) "Ruhmeshalle (Munich)"), which was completed in 1853.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-12)

#### Modernisation (1880–1900)

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modernisation (1880–1900)")]

Oktoberfest was cancelled for cholera epidemics (1854, 1873), the [Austro-Prussian War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") (1866) and the [Franco-Prussian War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") (1870).[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-13)

| Year | Milestone |
| --- | --- |
| 1880 | Electric lighting illuminated more than 400 booths and tents. |
| 1881 | The first bratwurst stalls opened. |
| 1887 | A brewery-dray parade became part of the official opening. |
| 1892 | Beer began to be served in glass mugs. |
| c. 1900 | Small booths were replaced by the large beer halls still used today.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-14) |

### 20th century

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 20th century")]

In 1910 the centenary celebration recorded the consumption of about 120,000 litres of beer. In 1913 the _Bräurosl_ pavilion opened, seating roughly 12,000 guests.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-15)

#### Interruptions

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Interruptions")]

*   1914–18 – Cancelled during World War I
*   1919–20 – Held only as a smaller _Kleineres Herbstfest_
*   1923–24 – Cancelled during hyperinflation

From 1933 to 1945 the Nazi regime used the festival for propaganda.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-16) In 1933 Jewish people were barred from working at the Wiesn.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-17) The festival was suspended 1939–45 during World War II; a modest “Autumn Fest” was held 1946–48.

Since 1950 the Mayor of Munich has opened Oktoberfest with a 12-gun salute and the cry “_[O'zapft is!](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/O%27zapft\_is! "O'zapft is!")_” (“It’s tapped!”).

[![Image 9](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Gamsbaerte_2194.jpg/250px-Gamsbaerte_2194.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Gamsbaerte_2194.jpg)

_[Gamsbärte](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gamsbart "Gamsbart")_ at the restaurateurs’ entry, 2008

#### 1980 bombing

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1980 bombing")]

Main article: [Oktoberfest bombing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_bombing "Oktoberfest bombing")

On 26 September 1980 a pipe bomb exploded near the main entrance, killing 13 people and injuring more than 225.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-18) It was the second-deadliest terrorist attack in Germany.

### 21st century

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In 2005 organisers introduced a “quiet Oktoberfest”: tents played only traditional brass music until 18:00, with afternoon volume capped at 85 dB.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-19)

A Bavarian smoking ban, fully enforced from 2011, made the festival smoke-free.[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-20)

[![Image 10](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Historische_Wiesen_2010.jpg/250px-Historische_Wiesen_2010.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Historische_Wiesen_2010.jpg)

Celebrating 200 years of Oktoberfest, 2010

The 200th anniversary in 2010 included a _historische Wiesn_ with a museum tent, special beer and a costumed horse race.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-21)

Attendance in 2013 reached 6.4 million, with 6.7 million litres of beer served.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-22)

The 2020 and 2021 festivals were cancelled because of the [COVID-19 pandemic in Germany](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Germany "COVID-19 pandemic in Germany").[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-23)[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-24)

On 1 October 2025 the festival was temporarily shut down because of an explosion in an area of [Munich](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Munich "Munich").[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-25)

## Annual traditions

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Annual traditions")]

### Opening parade

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Opening parade")]

The tradition of the Oktoberfest entry parade began in 1887, when Hans Steyrer, then a festival host, marched from his establishment on [Tegernseer Landstraße](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tegernseer_Landstra%C3%9Fe "Tegernseer Landstraße") to the _Theresienwiese_ with his staff, a brass band, and a cart of beer.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

In its current form, the parade has been held since 1935, when all participating breweries took part for the first time. Since 1950, the procession has been led by the [Münchner Kindl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M%C3%BCnchner_Kindl "Münchner Kindl"), followed by the incumbent [Mayor of Munich](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Munich "List of mayors of Munich") riding in the Schottenhammel family carriage. The parade also features decorated horse-drawn wagons and floats from the breweries, as well as carriages representing other restaurateurs and showpeople. Music bands from the beer tents accompany the procession.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-26)

[![Image 11](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Oktoberfest-Kutscher.jpg/250px-Oktoberfest-Kutscher.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Oktoberfest-Kutscher.jpg)

Coachmen in costume

### Official opening ceremony

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Official opening ceremony")]

Main article: [O'zapft is!](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/O%27zapft_is! "O'zapft is!")

Following the parade, the official opening of Oktoberfest takes place at exactly 12:00 p.m. in the Schottenhammel tent. The Mayor of Munich taps the first keg of beer and announces the Bavarian phrase "_O'zapft is!_" (_Es ist angezapft_ – "It is tapped!"). This marks the official start of the festival.

Twelve gunshots are then fired on the stairway of [Ruhmeshalle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruhmeshalle_(Munich) "Ruhmeshalle (Munich)"). This is the signal for the other restaurateurs to start with the serving of beer.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-27) Traditionally, the [Bavarian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria")[Minister-President](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minister-President "Minister-President") is served the first litre of beer. Then in the other tents, the first barrels are tapped and beer is served to the visitors.

Every year, visitors eagerly await to see how many strokes the mayor needs to use before the first beer flows. Bets are even made. The best performance is still two strokes ([Christian Ude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christian_Ude "Christian Ude"), 2005, 2008, 2009, 2010, 2011, 2012 and 2013; [Dieter Reiter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dieter_Reiter "Dieter Reiter"), 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018 and 2019),[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-28) and there was also 19 strokes required ([Thomas Wimmer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Wimmer_(politician) "Thomas Wimmer (politician)"), 1950).

### Costume and riflemen parade

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Costume and riflemen parade")]

The first costume parade was held in 1835 to mark the [silver wedding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wedding_anniversary "Wedding anniversary") anniversary of King [Ludwig I of Bavaria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria") and [Princess Therese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Therese_of_Saxe-Hildburghausen "Therese of Saxe-Hildburghausen"). A second parade followed in 1895, organised by the Bavarian novelist [Maximilian Schmidt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maximilian_Schmidt "Maximilian Schmidt") and involving about 1,400 participants in 150 costume groups.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-schmidt-29) A further parade was held during the centenary celebrations in 1910, directed by Julius and Moritz Wallach, early promoters of the [Dirndl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirndl "Dirndl") and [Lederhosen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lederhosen "Lederhosen") as everyday fashion.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-staendecke-30)[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-bielefeld-31)

[![Image 12](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/021_Trachtenzumzug_Beuerberg.JPG/330px-021_Trachtenzumzug_Beuerberg.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:021_Trachtenzumzug_Beuerberg.JPG)

Participants in the 2013 costume and riflemen parade

The modern parade has taken place each year since 1950—except in 2020–2021—and resumed in 2022.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-32) It is now a regular feature of Oktoberfest and is among the largest processions of its kind.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_] On the first Sunday of the festival roughly 8,000 participants walk the 7 km (4.3 mi) route from the [Maximilianeum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maximilianeum "Maximilianeum") to the Theresienwiese.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-33)

The procession is led by the _Münchner Kindl_, followed by members of the Munich city council, representatives of the Bavarian state government, musical and marching bands, traditional-costume and rifle clubs, flag-wavers, and about 40 decorated horse-drawn carriages. Most groups come from Bavaria, though delegations also arrive from other German states, [Austria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [Switzerland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), [Northern Italy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"), and other European regions.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-34) The entry of the _Wiesnwirte_ (festival innkeepers) and the costume and marksmen procession are organised by [Festring München](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Festring_M%C3%BCnchen "Festring München").[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-festring-history-35)

### Unofficial sport

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Unofficial sport")]

The unofficial sport of Oktoberfest is Masskrugstemmen, or [Steinholding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steinholding_(sport) "Steinholding (sport)"), in which competitors hold a filled one-liter dimpled glass mug with an outstretched arm for as long as they can.[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-36)

## Duration and dates

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Duration and dates")]

Since 1994, Oktoberfest has typically lasted 16 days, ending on the first Sunday in October. If that Sunday falls before 3 October ([German Unity Day](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_Unity_Day "German Unity Day")), the festival is extended through 3 October.

| Year | Dates | Duration | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2000 | 16 Sep – 3 Oct | 18 days | with ZLF |
| 2001 | 22 Sep – 7 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2002 | 21 Sep – 6 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2003 | 20 Sep – 5 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2004 | 18 Sep – 3 Oct | 16 days | with ZLF |
| 2005 | 17 Sep – 3 Oct | 17 days |  |
| 2006 | 16 Sep – 3 Oct | 18 days |  |
| 2007 | 22 Sep – 7 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2008 | 20 Sep – 5 Oct | 16 days | 175th Oktoberfest, with ZLF |
| 2009 | 19 Sep – 4 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2010 | 18 Sep – 4 Oct | 17 days | 200th anniversary, with ZLF |
| 2011 | 17 Sep – 3 Oct | 17 days |  |
| 2012 | 22 Sep – 7 Oct | 16 days | with ZLF |
| 2013 | 21 Sep – 6 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2014 | 20 Sep – 5 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2015 | 19 Sep – 4 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2016 | 17 Sep – 3 Oct | 17 days |  |
| 2017 | 16 Sep – 3 Oct | 18 days |  |
| 2018 | 22 Sep – 7 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2019 | 21 Sep – 6 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2020 | 19 Sep – 4 Oct | Cancelled | COVID-19 pandemic |
| 2021 | 18 Sep – 3 Oct | Cancelled | COVID-19 pandemic |
| 2022 | 17 Sep – 3 Oct | 17 days |  |
| 2023 | 16 Sep – 3 Oct | 18 days |  |
| 2024 | 21 Sep – 6 Oct | 16 days |  |
| 2025 | 20 Sep – 5 Oct | 16 days |  |

The _Bayerisches Zentral-Landwirtschaftsfest_ (Bavarian Central Agricultural Fair) is held every four years alongside Oktoberfest.

## Attendance

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Attendance")]

[![Image 13](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Hacker-Pschorr_Oktoberfest_Girl_Remix.jpg/330px-Hacker-Pschorr_Oktoberfest_Girl_Remix.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hacker-Pschorr_Oktoberfest_Girl_Remix.jpg)

A waitress serving a Maß of beer in a traditional Bavarian _Dirndl_

Oktoberfest is one of the largest festivals in the world, attracting millions of visitors annually. In 1999, about 6.5 million people visited the 42-hectare [Theresienwiese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theresienwiese "Theresienwiese") fairground.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-37) Around 72% of visitors came from Bavaria, and 15% from abroad, including neighbouring EU countries, North America, Oceania, and East Asia.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-38)

The grounds also host other major events such as the _Munich Frühlingsfest_ (April–May) and the [Tollwood Festival](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tollwood_Festival "Tollwood Festival") (December).

## Beer

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Beer")]

Only beer brewed within Munich’s city limits and in accordance with the [Reinheitsgebot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reinheitsgebot "Reinheitsgebot") may be sold at Oktoberfest.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-oktbeer-39) These beers are marketed under the protected designation "Oktoberfestbier".

### Styles

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Styles")]

Two types of beer are served:

*   The traditional _[Märzen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M%C3%A4rzen "Märzen")_ lager, formerly the standard.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-oktbeer-39)
*   The paler seasonal _Festbier_, which has become more common since the late 20th century.[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-40)

### Breweries

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Breweries")]

The right to produce Oktoberfestbier is held by the six breweries of the Club of Munich Brewers:[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-41)

*   [Augustiner Bräu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augustiner_Br%C3%A4u "Augustiner Bräu")
*   [Hacker-Pschorr](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hacker-Pschorr "Hacker-Pschorr")
*   [Löwenbräu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/L%C3%B6wenbr%C3%A4u "Löwenbräu")
*   [Paulaner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paulaner "Paulaner")
*   [Spaten](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spaten-Franziskaner-Br%C3%A4u "Spaten-Franziskaner-Bräu")
*   [Hofbräu München](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Staatliches_Hofbr%C3%A4uhaus_in_M%C3%BCnchen "Staatliches Hofbräuhaus in München")

## Tents

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Tents")]

Main article: [Oktoberfest tents](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_tents "Oktoberfest tents")

[![Image 14](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Theresienwiese_Hacker-Festzelt_Oktoberfest_2025.jpg/250px-Theresienwiese_Hacker-Festzelt_Oktoberfest_2025.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Theresienwiese_Hacker-Festzelt_Oktoberfest_2025.jpg)

Hacker-Festzelt in 2025

As of 2025, Oktoberfest includes 14 large and 20 small tents. They are temporary wooden structures built for use during the festival.[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-42)

Each large tent is associated with one of the Munich breweries, while a few smaller tents serve wine.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-43)

### Large tents

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Large tents")]

| Tent name | Brewery/operator | Seats (inside / outside) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Marstall | Spaten-Franziskaner-Bräu | 3,200 / 1,000 | Hosts evening performances, including _Münchner Zwietracht_ playing Oktoberfest music.[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-44) |
| Armbrustschützenzelt | Paulaner | 5,839 / 1,600 | Known as the Crossbowman’s Tent; crossbow competitions have been held here since 1895. |
| Hofbräu-Festzelt | Hofbräu München | 6,896 / 3,622 | The Hofbräu tent, popular with international visitors.[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-45) |
| Hacker-Festzelt | Hacker-Pschorr | 6,900 / 2,400 | Nicknamed _Himmel der Bayern_ (“Heaven of the Bavarians”); features rock music evenings. |
| Schottenhamel | Spaten-Franziskaner-Bräu | 6,000 / 4,000 | Site of the official keg tapping by the mayor; a popular student meeting point.[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-46)[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-47) |
| Winzerer Fähndl | Paulaner | 8,450 / 2,450 | Identified by its tower topped with a beer mug; named after a Bavarian military unit. |
| Schützen-Festhalle | Löwenbräu | 4,442 / 0 | Located beneath the Bavaria statue; current structure opened in 2004. |
| Käfer Wiesn-Schänke | Paulaner | 1,000 / 1,900 | The smallest large tent; offers gourmet food and opens later in the day. |
| Weinzelt | Nymphenburger Sekt / Paulaner Weißbier | 1,300 / 600 | Combines a wine bar (with over 15 varieties) and Paulaner wheat beer. |
| Löwenbräu-Festhalle | Löwenbräu | 5,700 / 2,800 | Features a large lion figure above the entrance and two beer towers. |
| Bräurosl | Hacker-Pschorr | 6,000 / 2,200 | Named after the brewer’s daughter; hosts the _Rosa Wiesn_ LGBT event. |
| Augustiner-Festhalle | Augustiner Bräu | 6,000 / 2,500 | Serves Augustiner beer from traditional wooden kegs. |
| Ochsenbraterei | Spaten | 5,900 / 1,500 | Specialises in roasted ox dishes. |
| Fischer-Vroni | Augustiner | 2,695 / 700 | Known for fish dishes, especially grilled _[Steckerlfisch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steckerlfisch "Steckerlfisch")_. |

### Small tents

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Small tents")]

| Tent name | Brewery/operator | Seats (inside / outside) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Able’s Kalbs-Kuchl | Spaten | 300 / 0 | Specialises in veal dishes in a rustic hut setting. |
| Ammer Hühner- & Entenbraterei | Augustiner | 450 / 450 | Established in 1885 as a chicken roastery; also serves duck. |
| Bodo’s Cafézelt | Independent | 450 / 0 | Serves cocktails, sparkling wine, coffee, pastries, and ice cream. |
| Café Kaiserschmarrn | Rischart | 400 / 0 | Operated by the Rischart bakery; themed around the royal wedding of 1810. |
| Café Mohrenkopf | Independent | 420 / 0 | Coffeehouse known for cakes and pies, operating since 1950. |
| Feisinger’s Ka’s und Weinstubn | Independent | 92 / 90 | Focuses on cheese dishes, wine, and wheat beer. |
| Glöckle Wirt | Spaten | 140 / 0 | Decorated with oil paintings and traditional instruments. |
| Heimer Hendl- & Entenbraterei | Paulaner | 400 / 0 | Family-run tent serving chicken and duck. |
| Heinz Wurst- & Hühnerbraterei | Paulaner | 360 / 0 | Specialises in sausages and chicken; operating since 1906. |
| Hochreiter’s Haxnbraterei | Löwenbräu | 250 / 0 | Known for pork knuckles, prepared in a dedicated roaster. |
| Münchner Knödelei | Paulaner | 300 / 90 | Dedicated to Bavarian dumpling dishes. |
| Poschner’s Hühner- & Entenbraterei | Hacker-Pschorr | 350 / 0 | Longstanding family-run chicken and duck roastery. |
| Schiebl’s Kaffeehaferl | Independent | 100 / 0 | Coffee tent; “Haferl” is a Bavarian term for mug or pot. |
| Wiesn Guglhupf Café-Dreh-Bar | Independent | 60 / 0 | Carousel bar shaped like a Guglhupf (bundt cake). |
| Wildmoser Hühnerbraterei | Hacker-Pschorr | 320 / 0 | Chicken roastery operated by the Wildmoser family since 1981. |
| Wildstuben | Augustiner | 271 / 0 | Built in hunting-lodge style with detailed woodwork. |
| Wirtshaus im Schichtl | Independent | 120 / 0 | Hosts daily cabaret-style shows unique to Oktoberfest. |
| Zum Stiftl | Paulaner | 360 / 0 | Serves roast duck and chicken with daily musical entertainment. |
| Zur Bratwurst | Augustiner | 160 / 0 | Opened in 2007, reviving the historic Bratwurstglöckl name. |

## Special events

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Special events")]

[![Image 15](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Historisches_Oktoberfest_2010_%285033833399%29.jpg/250px-Historisches_Oktoberfest_2010_%285033833399%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Historisches_Oktoberfest_2010_(5033833399).jpg)

Folk dancers at the historical Oktoberfest

### _Oide Wiesn_ (historical Oktoberfest)

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Oide Wiesn (historical Oktoberfest)")]

The historical Oktoberfest (_Oide Wiesn_, Bavarian for “old fairground”) was introduced in 2010 for the 200th anniversary of Oktoberfest. It was held on the former site of the Central Agricultural Festival (ZLF) at the south end of the [Theresienwiese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theresienwiese "Theresienwiese") and became a recurring feature from 2011.[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-oktoberfest-48)

The event opened one day before the main festival with the traditional keg tapping by the Lord Mayor.[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-schulte-49) In 2012, the Munich City Council set the 2013 entry fee at €3 with re-entry permitted, while historic rides cost €1 per use. Seating in the musicians’ tent was expanded, and a €200,000 grant supported the Showman Foundation’s museum tent, velodrome, and children’s programme.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-sued-50) Six Munich breweries presented historical dark beers, mugs bore the inscription "Münchner Bier" instead of logos, and closing time was set at 20:00. Attendance exceeded 500,000 in the first year, leading to temporary closures. The council also approved an _Oide Wiesn_ in 2015 before the return of the Central Agricultural Exhibition in 2016. [_[needs update](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual\_of\_Style/Dates\_and\_numbers#Chronological\_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")_]

The fenced grounds cover 2 [hectares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hectare "Hectare") (4.9 [acres](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acre "Acre")) and feature historic rides such as a carousel and chain swing, beer tents serving dark beer based on early 19th-century recipes, food stalls including _[Steckerlfisch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steckerlfisch "Steckerlfisch")_ grills, an animal tent and racecourse run by [Hellabrunn Zoo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hellabrunn_Zoo "Hellabrunn Zoo") and the Bavarian Farmers’ Association, and a museum tent curated by the [Munich Stadtmuseum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Munich_Stadtmuseum "Munich Stadtmuseum"). Cultural performances have included local groups such as the _[Biermösl Blosn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bierm%C3%B6sl\_Blosn "Biermösl Blosn")_.[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-oktoberfest-48)

### _Rosa Wiesn_ (Gay Oktoberfest)

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Rosa Wiesn (Gay Oktoberfest)")]

The _Rosa Wiesn_ (_Pink Wiesn_), also called Gay Oktoberfest, is a series of [LGBT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") events held during Oktoberfest. The main gathering, Gay Sunday, takes place in the [Bräurosl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_tents "Oktoberfest tents") tent on the first Sunday.[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-51)[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-52)[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-53)

[![Image 16](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Mvgphoto_Bierfest.jpg/250px-Mvgphoto_Bierfest.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Mvgphoto_Bierfest.jpg)

Rosa Wiesn attendees in 2016

The tradition began in the 1970s when members of the _Münchner Löwen Club_ (MLC) reserved the Bräurosl balcony and were mistaken for a football club. The event became an annual gathering.[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-54)

Alongside Gay Sunday, the programme includes meet-and-greets, _\_Löwennacht\__ (Lion’s Night), brunches, and cultural activities. About 8,000 visitors attend each year.[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-55) Some sources describe it as Germany’s second-largest LGBT gathering after [Christopher Street Day](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christopher_Street_Day "Christopher Street Day").[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_][[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-56)

## Public safety

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Public safety")]

In 2004 the _Behördenhof_ (“authorities’ court”) was established as a service hub for police, fire, medical, and administrative coordination. A temporary police station operates there during the festival.

Since 2005 officers from [Bolzano](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bolzano "Bolzano"), Italy, have assisted with crowd management and communication, especially on weekends with high numbers of Italian visitors. The _Behördenhof_, on the eastern side of the _Theresienwiese_, functions as the main command center.

[![Image 17](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Beh%C3%B6rdenhof_Oktoberfest_Muenchenjpg.jpg/250px-Beh%C3%B6rdenhof_Oktoberfest_Muenchenjpg.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Beh%C3%B6rdenhof_Oktoberfest_Muenchenjpg.jpg)

Service hub (Behördenhof) on the Theresienwiese, established 2004

[![Image 18](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/2010-09-20_WiesnSicherheit_8441.jpg/250px-2010-09-20_WiesnSicherheit_8441.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:2010-09-20_WiesnSicherheit_8441.jpg)

Police surveillance during Oktoberfest, 2010

Medical care is provided by the Bavarian branch of the [German Red Cross](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_Red_Cross "German Red Cross"), with about 100 volunteer doctors and paramedics on duty each day.[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-57) Aicher Ambulance operates an additional post in the _Fischer Vroni_ tent. The service complex includes an operating theatre and treatment rooms, and ambulances are stationed at key points across the grounds.

Visitor services at the _Behördenhof_ include a child reunification point, a lost property office, and a women’s support center. Further assistance is available at the nearby _Theresienwiese_[U-Bahn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Munich_U-Bahn "Munich U-Bahn") station, staffed by [Johanniter-Unfall-Hilfe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Johanniter-Unfall-Hilfe "Johanniter-Unfall-Hilfe").[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-58)

### Preventive measures

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Preventive measures")]

In 2003, the campaign _Sichere Wiesn für Mädchen und Frauen_ (“Safe Oktoberfest for Girls and Women”) was launched to prevent sexual violence and abuse against women during the event.

In 2010, animals were banned from the festival grounds. In 2012, glass bottles were prohibited after an increase in injuries caused by broken glass.

### Ride safety

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Ride safety")]

All rides are inspected before the festival by the cableways and temporary structures division of _[TÜV SÜD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%C3%9CV\_S%C3%9CD "TÜV SÜD")_.

On 30 September 1996, 30 people were injured in a collision on the [Euro Star](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Euro_Star_(roller_coaster)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Euro Star (roller coaster) (page does not exist)") roller coaster. The cause was a worn safety brake not detected during inspection. Prosecutors in Munich investigated an engineer from TÜV Munich for negligent bodily harm, but no conviction followed.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-59)

### Security developments

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Security developments")]

*   1981 – Main entrance redesigned after the [1980 bombing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_terror_attack "Oktoberfest terror attack").
*   2001 – Permanent checkpoints introduced following the [September 11 attacks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").
*   2008 – _Theresienwiese_ closed to the public during construction.
*   2009 – Roadblocks and stricter access controls introduced in response to security concerns.
*   2010 – Three security rings, flight restrictions, and 52 concrete bollards added.[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-60)
*   2011 – 170 retractable bollards installed, the [Bavariaring](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bavariaring&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bavariaring (page does not exist)") emergency corridor widened, and authorities given powers to redirect crowds or close rail stations.[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-61)[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-62)
*   2016 – A retractable fence enclosed the final 350 m (1,150 ft) of perimeter. Security staff were stationed at 13 entrances. Bags over 3 L (0.66 imp gal; 0.79 US gal) were banned, and the _Theresienwiese_[U-Bahn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Munich_U-Bahn "Munich U-Bahn") front exit was closed.[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-63)

## Infrastructure

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Electricity

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Electricity")]

Oktoberfest is supplied with electricity through 43 km (27 mi) of cable connected to 18 partly underground transformer stations. They provide about 2.9 million kilowatt-hours per year, excluding the energy required for assembly and dismantling.[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-power-64) Most tents and rides use certified renewable electricity from Stadtwerke München to reduce local emissions.

The electrical grid is designed with redundancy: every circuit can be supplied by an independent line, and each tent is connected to two substations.

#### Notable incident

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Notable incident")]

On 25 September 2007, heavy rain flooded an underground cable duct, interrupting power for several hours and delaying morning food service.[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-65)

### Natural gas

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Natural gas")]

A 4 km (2.5 mi) distribution network supplies gas to the festival grounds. Kitchens consume about 159,000 m³ each season, and outdoor heaters about 42,000 m³.[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-power-64)

### Telecommunications

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Telecommunications")]

To maintain mobile phone coverage in the dense crowds, network operators install temporary masts on the surrounding lawns before each festival.

## Transportation

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Oktoberfest attracts millions of visitors, and the city provides a range of transport options to manage the crowds. Most people arrive by public transport, while others come on foot, by car, or by taxi. Special measures are introduced each year to handle congestion, parking, and safety.

### Public transport

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Public transport")]

[![Image 19](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Oktoberfest_UBahnTheresienwiese.JPG/250px-Oktoberfest_UBahnTheresienwiese.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Oktoberfest_UBahnTheresienwiese.JPG)

[Theresienwiese station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theresienwiese_station "Theresienwiese station") during Oktoberfest

The [Münchner Verkehrsgesellschaft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M%C3%BCnchner_Verkehrsgesellschaft "Münchner Verkehrsgesellschaft") (Munich Transport Company) estimates that about four million trips are made to and from the grounds each year. The [U-Bahn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U-Bahn_M%C3%BCnchen "U-Bahn München") and [S-Bahn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S-Bahn_M%C3%BCnchen "S-Bahn München") are heavily used, especially at night. The nearest underground stop, [Theresienwiese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theresienwiese_station "Theresienwiese station"), runs at three-minute intervals during peak times. After the tents close, the station may be closed temporarily to prevent overcrowding. Extra security staff are deployed by both the transport operator and [Deutsche Bahn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn").

Nearby stations include [Goetheplatz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goetheplatz_(Munich_U-Bahn) "Goetheplatz (Munich U-Bahn)"), [Schwanthalerhöhe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schwanthalerh%C3%B6he_(Munich_U-Bahn) "Schwanthalerhöhe (Munich U-Bahn)"), and [Hackerbrücke](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Munich_Hackerbr%C3%BCcke_station "Munich Hackerbrücke station") (S-Bahn). Many visitors also walk from the nearby [main railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen_Hauptbahnhof "München Hauptbahnhof").

### Road traffic and parking

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Road traffic and parking")]

Road traffic is often congested during the festival. The [Bavarian State Police](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bavarian_State_Police "Bavarian State Police") conduct large-scale [DUI checks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Driving_under_the_influence "Driving under the influence") and restrict lanes on major roads and highways. Parking close to the grounds is limited, and towing is common.

During the middle weekend, when many Italian visitors arrive with [caravans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Travel_trailer "Travel trailer"), the city enforces camping bans in central areas and provides remote parking sites, such as near the [Allianz Arena](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allianz_Arena "Allianz Arena"), with public transport connections.

### Taxis

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Taxis")]

Since 2010, taxi stands have been located outside the secure perimeter, farther from the festival grounds.

## Sanitation

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Sanitation")]

Oktoberfest organizers implement strict [waste management](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waste_management "Waste management") and restroom policies to maintain hygiene and safety on the festival grounds.

### Waste management

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Waste management")]

All public trash bins were removed after the [1980 bombing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_bombing "Oktoberfest bombing") to reduce the risk of concealed explosives.[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-66) Waste collection is instead handled through centralized disposal systems and frequent cleaning by sanitation teams.

### Restrooms

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Restrooms")]

By 2004, queueing delays at toilets had grown so severe that police managed access. To reduce congestion, patrons needing only to urinate are directed to [urinals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urinal "Urinal") consisting of large sheltered grates. In 2005, overall capacity was expanded by 20%, and today about 1,800 toilets and urinals are available on the grounds.[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-67)

In 2005, organizers considered restricting mobile phone use in restrooms through [Faraday cage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Faraday_cage "Faraday cage") installations and [jammers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phone_jammer "Phone jammer"). These measures were abandoned because jammers are illegal in Germany and cages proved too costly. Instead, signage requests that patrons refrain from phone use inside restrooms.[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-68) In later years, background music was introduced near facilities to discourage prolonged telephone use.[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-69)

## Other Oktoberfest celebrations

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Other Oktoberfest celebrations")]

Main article: [Oktoberfest celebrations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_celebrations "Oktoberfest celebrations")

Several large folk festivals worldwide follow the model of Munich’s Oktoberfest:

*   **[Qingdao International Beer Festival](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qingdao_International_Beer_Festival "Qingdao International Beer Festival")** (Qingdao, China) – approximately 3 million visitors annually
*   **[Kitchener–Waterloo Oktoberfest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kitchener%E2%80%93Waterloo_Oktoberfest "Kitchener–Waterloo Oktoberfest")** (Kitchener, Canada) – around 700,000 visitors annually
*   **[Blumenau Oktoberfest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blumenau_Oktoberfest "Blumenau Oktoberfest")** (Blumenau, Brazil) – about 600,000 visitors annually
*   **[Oktoberfest Zinzinnati](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_Zinzinnati "Oktoberfest Zinzinnati")** (Cincinnati, United States) – held since 1976 in honour of the city’s German heritage; over 500,000 visitors, the largest in the United States.[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-70)
*   **Viennese Wiesn** ([Vienna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), Austria) – inaugural event held 23 September–2 October 2011 on the Kaiserwiese, attended by about 150,000 visitors in three tents.[[71]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-71)

Festivals inspired by Oktoberfest are also held in Australia, Russia, Namibia and Japan.

### German variants

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: German variants")]

In Germany itself, many cities host their own Oktoberfest-style events:

*   **[Oktoberfest Hannover](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_Hannover "Oktoberfest Hannover")[[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest_Hannover "de:Oktoberfest Hannover")]** – approximately 500,000 visitors, the second-largest Oktoberfest in Germany

## In popular culture

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

A German historical drama called _Oktoberfest: Beer and Blood_ was released in 2020.[[72]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-72) Set in 1900, it focuses on the showman brewer Curt Prank as he transforms the festival into a global tourist attraction by replacing the local brewery stands with one large pavilion.[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-73) Critics have compared the show's graphic violence and German [new wave music](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music") soundtrack to _[Peaky Blinders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peaky\_Blinders\_(TV\_series) "Peaky Blinders (TV series)")_.[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-74) A second season was announced by head writer Ronny Schalk in 2021.[[75]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_note-75)

## See also

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: See also")]

*   [Beer and Oktoberfest Museum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beer_and_Oktoberfest_Museum "Beer and Oktoberfest Museum")
*   [Schunkeln](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schunkeln "Schunkeln") (sway dance)
*   [Steinholding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steinholding_(sport) "Steinholding (sport)") (sport)

## References

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: References")]

73.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_ref-73)**[Inverse](https://www.inverse.com/entertainment/oktoberfest-beer-blood-builds-on-the-ambitious-legacy-of-dark)
74.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_ref-74)**["Netflix made a drama about Oktoberfest and it's a deliciously dark devil's brew"](https://www.cnet.com/news/netflix-oktoberfest-drama-tastes-like-peaky-blinders-beer-and-blood/). _[CNET](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CNET "CNET")_. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20211126042051/https://www.cnet.com/news/netflix-oktoberfest-drama-tastes-like-peaky-blinders-beer-and-blood/) from the original on 26 November 2021.
75.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest#cite_ref-75)**[Oktoberfest Season 2 release](https://www.inverse.com/entertainment/oktoberfest-beer-blood-season-2-release-date-trailer-renewal)

## External links

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Oktoberfest&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: External links")]

Wikimedia Commons has media related to [Oktoberfest](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Oktoberfest "commons:Oktoberfest").

Listen to this article (15 minutes)

![Image 21: Spoken Wikipedia icon](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

[This audio file](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:En-Oktoberfest-article.ogg "File:En-Oktoberfest-article.ogg") was created from a revision of this article dated 26 September 2013(2013-09-26), and does not reflect subsequent edits.
